The client MUST send a Confirm message when it detects a network information change (as specified in Section 18.2.12) and has only DHCP-assigned addresses (without any delegated prefixes) to verify whether it remains connected to the same link. This message exchange is initiated when the client suspects a potential link change but needs to validate the continued validity of its current address assignments. The client MUST include a Client Identifier option to identify itself and IA options containing all currently assigned addresses for the interface, while setting T1/T2 and lifetime fields to 0 since the server ignores these values. The first Confirm message MUST be delayed by a random time between 0 and CNF_MAX_DELAY, and transmission follows the retransmission parameters: IRT=CNF_TIMEOUT, MRT=CNF_MAX_RT, MRC=0, MRD=CNF_MAX_RD. If no response is received after retransmission completes, the client SHOULD continue using existing leases with their last known lifetimes and previously obtained configuration parameters.

When constructing a Confirm message to verify link connectivity after detecting network changes, the client SHOULD set the T1 and T2 fields in all IA_NA options (Section 21.4) and the preferred-lifetime and valid-lifetime fields in all IA Address options (Section 21.6) to zero. This applies specifically during Confirm message creation when the client has active IA_NA bindings and needs to validate whether previously assigned addresses remain valid on the current link. The server will ignore these lifetime values in Confirm messages and instead determine address validity based on the client's current network attachment. Following this guidance ensures efficient protocol operation by avoiding unnecessary transmission of ignored parameters while maintaining compliance with the DHCPv6 specification.

When a DHCPv6 client sends a Confirm message to verify its network connectivity after detecting a link change, it MUST include IA options for all Identity Association (IA) instances currently assigned to the interface triggering the Confirm message. Each IA option MUST contain all addresses currently associated with that IA, but SHOULD set T1/T2 fields in IA_NA options and preferred/valid-lifetime fields in IA Address options to 0, as these values are ignored by the server during confirmation. This rule applies specifically when the client has obtained addresses (without delegated prefixes) from a DHCP server and needs to validate whether it remains connected to the same link. The inclusion of all assigned IAs ensures the server can properly validate the client's current address assignments against the correct network context.
req_fields: IA_NA options with T1/T2 fields set to 0
IA Address options with preferred/valid-lifetime fields set to 0

rule: When a DHCPv6 client detects a network change and needs to verify link connectivity using a Confirm message (as specified in Section 18.2.12), the client MUST delay the transmission of the first Confirm message on the interface by a randomly selected duration between 0 and CNF_MAX_DELAY. This delay applies specifically to the initial Confirm message in the exchange and serves to prevent synchronization effects and reduce network congestion when multiple clients simultaneously detect network changes. The rule applies after the client has constructed the Confirm message with all required options (Client Identifier, Elapsed Time, and relevant IA options) but before initiating the transmission procedure defined in Section 15. Following this delay, the client proceeds with the message transmission using the retransmission parameters IRT=CNF_TIMEOUT, MRT=CNF_MAX_RT, MRC=0, and MRD=CNF_MAX_RD.

When a DHCPv6 client initiates a Rebind message exchange after reaching time T2 without receiving a response to its Renew message, or when verifying delegated prefix bindings, the client must construct the Rebind message without including the Server Identifier option (Section 21.3). This applies specifically during the Rebind/Reply exchange phase when communicating with any available server, not just the original server. The omission of the Server Identifier option indicates that the client is seeking service from any responding DHCPv6 server, which distinguishes Rebind messages from Renew messages that target a specific server. This behavior ensures proper protocol operation when the original server becomes unavailable and facilitates server discovery through broadcast communication.

When the client reaches time T2 without receiving a response to its Renew message sent at time T1, it initiates a Rebind/Reply exchange with any available server to extend its lease lifetimes. The client constructs the Rebind message following the same general message construction guidelines specified in Section 18.2.4, but with these specific modifications: the client MUST set the "msg-type" field to REBIND and MUST NOT include the Server Identifier option. This rule applies specifically when the original delegating server becomes unresponsive, allowing the client to broadcast its request to any available server while maintaining the same IA bindings. The client then transmits the message using the retransmission parameters defined for Rebind messages (IRT REB_TIMEOUT, MRT REB_MAX_RT, MRC 0, MRD remaining lease time).

When initiating a DHCPv6 configuration exchange, during the creation of a Solicit message as specified in Section 18.2.1, the client MUST generate a unique transaction ID and populate the "transaction-id" field with this value. This applies specifically to the initial message in the DHCPv6 client-server exchange and serves to correlate requests with responses, ensuring proper message association throughout the transaction lifecycle. The transaction ID MUST be generated before any other message field population and MUST remain consistent for all messages within the same transaction sequence. This rule is mandatory for all Solicit message transmissions and is a prerequisite for valid DHCPv6 protocol operation.

When creating a Request message to obtain leases and configuration information from a DHCPv6 server, the client MUST include one or more Identity Association (IA) options to specify which address or prefix assignments it is requesting. This requirement applies after the client has selected a server (typically following an Advertise message exchange) and is initiating the configuration phase. The inclusion of IA options enables the server to identify the specific address/prefix resources being requested and respond with appropriate lease assignments in the Reply message. The client must have already generated a valid transaction ID and included mandatory options (Client Identifier, Server Identifier, Elapsed Time, and Option Request options) before transmitting the Request message with the required IA options.

After a client sends a Request message containing one or more IA options to populate IAs with leases and obtain configuration information, the server MUST respond with a Reply message containing IA options that include the assigned leases and associated configuration information for those IAs. This rule applies specifically during the DHCPv6 Request-Reply exchange when the client has successfully identified the target server through a Server Identifier option and authenticated itself via a Client Identifier option. The expected outcome is that the client receives the requested lease assignments and configuration parameters, enabling it to complete its network configuration. This exchange occurs only after the client has completed server discovery and selection phases, and the server has validated the client's request and has available resources to fulfill the IA assignments.

When a DHCPv6 client initiates a Solicit message to discover available DHCP servers, it MUST include a Client Identifier option (see Section 21.2) to uniquely identify itself to the server. This requirement applies during the initial message construction phase before transmission, and the Client Identifier option must be present in every Solicit message regardless of the client's configuration state or requested parameters. The expected outcome is that servers can properly identify and track the client across subsequent message exchanges, enabling consistent address assignment and lease management. This rule applies unconditionally to all Solicit messages and is a prerequisite for valid protocol operation.

When constructing a Request message to populate Identity Associations (IAs) with leases and obtain configuration information, the client MUST include the identifier of the destination server in a Server Identifier option (see Section 21.3). This requirement applies after the client has selected a server (typically following receipt of Advertise messages) and is preparing to send the Request message. The Server Identifier option enables the receiving server to identify itself and properly associate the request with the client's previous interactions. This inclusion is mandatory regardless of whether the client is communicating with a new server or a previously known server, and must be accompanied by a Client Identifier option to establish mutual identification between the client and server.

When the client reaches time T2 without receiving a response to its Renew message sent at time T1, it initiates a Rebind/Reply exchange with any available server. The client constructs the Rebind message as specified in Section 18.2.4, setting the msg-type to REBIND and omitting the Server Identifier option. The message must be transmitted according to the retransmission mechanism in Section 15, with the following specific parameters: IRT (Initial Retransmission Time) set to REB_TIMEOUT, MRT (Maximum Retransmission Time) set to REB_MAX_RT, MRC (Maximum Retransmission Count) set to 0 (indicating unlimited retransmissions), and MRD (Maximum Retransmission Duration) set to the remaining time until all leases in all IA(s) expire. This exchange continues until either a Reply is received or all leases expire, at which point the client must transition to using Solicit messages to locate a new DHCP server.

When constructing a Request message to obtain leases and configuration information, the client MUST include all required options (Server Identifier, Client Identifier, Elapsed Time, and Option Request options) and SHOULD additionally include any other relevant options that support the configuration exchange. This MUST include one or more IA (Identity Association) options to specify which address/prefix assignments the client is requesting, and MAY include optional parameters such as the Reconfigure Accept option if supported. These additional options provide the server with complete information about the client's configuration requirements and preferences. This rule applies after the client has set the message type to REQUEST, generated a transaction ID, and included all mandatory options, but before the message is transmitted according to the specified retransmission parameters.

When constructing a Solicit message during the initial DHCPv6 configuration exchange, the client MUST include an Elapsed Time option (Section 21.9) that measures the cumulative duration from the start of the current DHCP message exchange attempt. This requirement applies specifically to the Solicit message transmission phase and serves to inform DHCPv6 servers about the client's waiting time, enabling servers to prioritize responses for clients that have been waiting longer. The client must calculate the elapsed time starting from when it began attempting to complete the current DHCP exchange, and this option must be included regardless of whether this is the initial transmission or a retransmission. This rule applies unconditionally to all Solicit messages and is essential for proper server load balancing and client prioritization in the DHCPv6 protocol.

When constructing a Request message to obtain leases and configuration parameters from a DHCPv6 server, the client MUST include an Option Request option (Section 21.7) that explicitly requests the SOL_MAX_RT option (Section 21.24) to receive the maximum retransmission time value, along with any other configuration options the client requires. This requirement applies during the Request message creation phase, after including mandatory Server Identifier and Client Identifier options but before message transmission. The expected outcome is that the server will respond with the requested options in its Reply message, enabling proper configuration parameter negotiation. This rule applies unconditionally to all Request messages and is essential for ensuring the client receives critical timing parameters and other necessary configuration data from the server.

During the creation of a Solicit message, after including the mandatory Option Request option (ORO) to specify which configuration options the client wishes to receive from the server, the client MAY optionally include instances of those same requested options with preferred data values as hints. These hint values provide guidance to the server about the specific parameter values the client would prefer to have returned in the server's response. This optional hint mechanism applies only to options that are explicitly listed in the ORO and must not include any other options except those specifically permitted by the protocol specification. The server may use these hints when formulating its response but is not required to honor them.
req_fields: requested options with preferred data values as hints

rule: During normal address and prefix lifecycle management, when a client detects potential movement to a new link (such as through network interface changes or connectivity shifts), it must select the appropriate message type based on its current resource allocation. If the client only possesses assigned addresses (IA_NA), it MUST use a Confirm message to verify address validity on the new link. If the client holds delegated prefixes (IA_PD), either alone or in combination with addresses, it MUST use a Rebind message instead. This distinction ensures proper resource validation and lease management during network transitions, maintaining protocol efficiency and preventing resource conflicts.

During the creation of a Solicit message in the DHCPv6 configuration exchange, the client MUST include a Reconfigure Accept option (see Section 21.20) if it is willing to accept Reconfigure messages from the server. This rule applies specifically when the client is preparing the initial Solicit message to discover available DHCPv6 servers, and indicates the client's capability to receive unsolicited Reconfigure messages that may trigger configuration updates. The inclusion of this option is conditional upon the client's configuration and policy regarding server-initiated reconfiguration. When included, it signals to servers that the client can process Reconfigure messages, potentially enabling more efficient configuration updates without requiring client-initiated renewal exchanges. The client MUST NOT include this option if it does not support or is not configured to accept Reconfigure messages.

During Solicit, Request, Renew, or Rebind message exchanges, when a client requests multiple IA option types (such as IA_NA and IA_PD) or multiple instances of the same IA type, available DHCPv6 servers may only be configured to provide a subset of the requested configuration. In such cases, the client SHOULD accept and utilize the available configuration while continuing to request the missing IAs in subsequent messages. This approach allows the client to maintain a single session and state machine while progressively obtaining its full configuration. The client should persistently include all desired configuration options in each subsequent exchange, as this situation typically results from temporary operational conditions rather than permanent limitations.

In the DHCPv6 protocol, a client MUST use Information-request messages specifically when it requires configuration parameters (such as DNS servers, domain names, or other network settings) but does not need stateful address assignment (IA_NA) or prefix delegation (IA_PD). This applies during initial configuration acquisition when the client already has valid IPv6 addresses through other mechanisms (such as SLAAC) or when responding to a Reconfigure message that indicates an Information-request is required. The expected outcome is that DHCPv6 servers will respond with a Reply message containing only the requested configuration options, maintaining the client's existing address and prefix assignments. This behavior is distinct from other message types like Solicit, Request, Renew, or Rebind, which are used when the client requires address or prefix management.

When a DHCPv6 client receives a valid Reconfigure message from a server, it SHOULD process this message by behaving as if its T1 renewal timer had expired, regardless of the actual timer state. This applies specifically during the address/prefix lifecycle management phase when the client needs to respond to server-initiated reconfiguration requests. The expected outcome is that the client will initiate a Renew message exchange to obtain updated configuration parameters from the server, while maintaining all currently held resources and continuing to validate the authenticity of the Reconfigure message. This behavior applies only when the client has an established binding with the server and the Reconfigure message passes basic validation checks.

When a DHCPv6 client receives a valid Reconfigure message from a server, it MUST respond with either a Renew, Rebind, or Information-request message as specified by the msg-type value in the Reconfigure Message option (see Section 21.19). This rule applies specifically during the client's operational state when it has an established binding with the server and the server needs to initiate a configuration update. The client MUST maintain all previously obtained resources and SHOULD treat the Reconfigure message as equivalent to a T1 timer expiration. This response mechanism enables efficient server-initiated configuration updates while maintaining session continuity. The client SHOULD validate the authenticity of Reconfigure messages to prevent potential spoofing attacks.

When a client receives a Reconfigure message from a DHCPv6 server, it MUST respond with a Renew, Rebind, or Information-request message as indicated by the Reconfigure Message option. However, since Reconfigure messages could potentially be spoofed by malicious actors, the client SHOULD treat such messages with suspicion and verify their authenticity through normal protocol mechanisms. Regardless of the Reconfigure message's validity, the client MUST NOT release or abandon any previously acquired IP addresses, delegated prefixes, or other configuration resources. The client SHOULD process the Reconfigure message as if the T1 timer had expired, triggering a configuration renewal while maintaining all existing resources. This ensures operational continuity while protecting against potential denial-of-service attacks through fake Reconfigure messages.

When a DHCPv6 client requests multiple IA option types or multiple instances of the same IA types in Solicit, Request, Renew, or Rebind messages, and receives only a subset of the requested configuration from available servers, the client SHOULD continue to request all originally programmed configuration information in each subsequent configuration exchange. This includes persistently requesting any stateful configuration options (such as IA_NA or IA_PD) that were not fulfilled in previous exchanges. This behavior increases the likelihood of eventually obtaining the complete configuration while maintaining a single session and state machine, particularly when partial responses result from temporary operational errors or server misconfigurations. The client should utilize the best available configuration received while continuing to request missing elements in follow-up exchanges.
req_fields: multiple instances of the same IA types

rule: After a client sends a Decline message to indicate that a previously assigned IPv6 address is no longer usable, upon receiving any valid Reply message from the server in response, the client MUST consider the Decline operation as completed. This completion is unconditional and applies irrespective of any Status Code option values contained in the server's Reply, ensuring the client proceeds with subsequent DHCPv6 operations without waiting for specific server acknowledgment of the declined address state.

During the DHCPv6 address lifecycle, when a client needs to send Request, Renew, Release, or Decline messages, it SHOULD use unicast transmission directly to the server if both of the following conditions are met: (1) the client possesses a source address with sufficient scope for the server to use as a return address, and (2) the client has previously received a Server Unicast option (Section 21.12) from that server. This unicast behavior applies after the initial discovery phase and enables more efficient communication by avoiding relay agent delays and reducing server overhead from multicast delivery. However, this approach should only be used when relay agent options are not required, as unicast transmission bypasses relay agents that would normally add these options.

In the DHCPv6 protocol, after a client sends a Release message to relinquish one or more IPv6 addresses, the client must consider the Release operation complete upon receiving any valid Reply message from the server—even if the server includes a Status Code option (as defined in Section 21.13) indicating an error or other status. This rule ensures that the client proceeds with address cleanup and state transition without interpreting server status codes in Release transactions, thereby maintaining protocol consistency and preventing potential state mismatches between client and server. The rule applies only when the Reply message is valid and directly corresponds to a previously transmitted Release message.

When a client has a valid source address of sufficient scope and has received a Server Unicast option from the server, it SHOULD unicast Request, Renew, Release, and Decline messages directly to the server to avoid relay-induced delays and reduce server overhead. However, if network policy requires the use of relay agent options for all client messages, the client MUST relay all DHCP messages through a relay agent. This requirement applies during the client's normal address lifecycle operations (Request, Renew, Release, Decline) and ensures that relay agent options can be consistently included in all client-originated messages. The server should only permit unicast operation when relay agent options are not required for the client's configuration.

After a client sends a Request, Renew, Rebind, or Information-request message (or responds to a Reconfigure message), it must expect the server to include Identity Associations (IAs) for address/prefix assignment and/or other configuration parameters (such as DNS servers or domain names) in the corresponding Reply message. This expectation applies during all stateful and stateless configuration exchanges where the client has explicitly requested resources or configuration data. The client should process the received IAs and configuration information according to their respective option formats and update its network configuration accordingly. This rule assumes the client has previously initiated a valid DHCPv6 exchange and is awaiting server response, and it does not apply to Solicit messages where servers respond with Advertise messages instead.
res_fields: other configuration parameters (such as DNS servers or domain names)

rule: When a DHCPv6 client receives a Reply message in response to an Information-request, it MUST follow the procedures specified in Section 21.23 for handling the Information Refresh Time option. This requirement applies irrespective of whether the Reply message actually contains the Information Refresh Time option. The client must determine the appropriate refresh timing behavior based on the presence or absence of the option as defined in Section 21.23, ensuring proper management of configuration information validity periods. This rule governs the client's refresh timing mechanism specifically for Information-request/Reply exchanges and is mandatory for all compliant implementations.

When initiating a DHCPv6 configuration exchange, during the creation of a Solicit message as specified in Section 18.2.1, the client MUST generate a unique transaction ID and populate the "transaction-id" field with this value. This applies specifically to the initial message in the DHCPv6 client-server exchange and serves to correlate requests with responses, ensuring proper message association throughout the transaction lifecycle. The transaction ID MUST be generated before any other message field population and MUST remain consistent for all messages within the same transaction sequence. This rule is mandatory for all Solicit message transmissions and is a prerequisite for valid DHCPv6 protocol operation.

When constructing an Information-request message to obtain configuration information without requesting address assignments, the client SHOULD include a Client Identifier option (see Section 21.2) to enable server identification and facilitate the return of client-specific configuration options. This applies during the initial message creation phase before transmission. Including the identifier allows servers to provide tailored responses based on client identity and state. However, clients may omit this option in privacy-sensitive scenarios as described in RFC7844 Section 4.3.1, recognizing that servers may either respond with generic configuration only or choose not to respond at all. The client MUST ensure all other required options (Elapsed Time and Option Request) are properly included regardless of the Client Identifier inclusion decision.

When a DHCPv6 client sends an Information-request message to obtain configuration information without address assignment, it SHOULD include a Client Identifier option to identify itself to the server. This rule applies during the message construction phase before transmission. If the client omits this option, the server cannot associate the request with specific client configuration data and therefore cannot return client-specific options. Additionally, the server may exercise its discretion to ignore the message entirely and not send any response. This behavior is particularly relevant when the client prioritizes privacy over personalized configuration, as permitted by RFC7844. The client must still include mandatory options such as Elapsed Time and Option Request options regardless of whether the Client Identifier is present.

When constructing a Solicit message during the initial DHCPv6 configuration exchange, the client MUST include an Elapsed Time option (Section 21.9) that measures the cumulative duration from the start of the current DHCP message exchange attempt. This requirement applies specifically to the Solicit message transmission phase and serves to inform DHCPv6 servers about the client's waiting time, enabling servers to prioritize responses for clients that have been waiting longer. The client must calculate the elapsed time starting from when it began attempting to complete the current DHCP exchange, and this option must be included regardless of whether this is the initial transmission or a retransmission. This rule applies unconditionally to all Solicit messages and is essential for proper server load balancing and client prioritization in the DHCPv6 protocol.

When constructing an Information-request message to obtain configuration information without address assignment, the client MUST include an Option Request option (see Section 21.7) that explicitly requests the INF_MAX_RT option (see Section 21.25) and Information Refresh Time option (see Section 21.23), in addition to any other configuration options the client requires. This requirement applies during the initial message creation phase before transmission and ensures the server provides critical timing parameters and other requested configuration data. The client must have already generated a transaction ID and included mandatory options (Elapsed Time option) before including the Option Request option. Following this rule enables the client to receive necessary server parameters for proper protocol operation and configuration management.

The client uses an Information-request message to obtain configuration information (such as DNS servers, domain names, or other network parameters) without requesting IP address assignment or delegated prefixes. This applies when the client already has valid addresses through other mechanisms (like SLAAC or static configuration) but requires additional network configuration. The client MUST set the message type to INFORMATION-REQUEST, generate a unique transaction ID, include an Elapsed Time option, and specify requested options via an Option Request option. When responding to a Reconfigure message, the client MUST include the Server Identifier option from that message. The first Information-request on an interface MUST be delayed by 0 to INF_MAX_DELAY seconds, and retransmission follows the parameters IRT=INF_TIMEOUT, MRT=INF_MAX_RT, MRC=0, MRD=0. The client SHOULD include a Client Identifier option for client-specific configuration, though omitting it may limit server responses.

When a DHCPv6 client responds to a Reconfigure message by sending an Information-request message, the client MUST include a Server Identifier option (see Section 21.3) containing the same server identifier value that was present in the received Reconfigure message. This requirement applies specifically when the Information-request is triggered by a Reconfigure message from the server, ensuring the client properly identifies which server's reconfiguration request it is responding to. The client must extract the server identifier from the Reconfigure message and copy it into the Server Identifier option of the Information-request response. This rule does not apply to Information-request messages initiated independently by the client without a preceding Reconfigure trigger.

When a DHCPv6 client initiates configuration information retrieval using an Information-request message on an interface, the initial transmission MUST be delayed by a random duration between 0 and INF_MAX_DELAY. This rule applies specifically to the first Information-request message sent on that interface during the current configuration exchange, and serves to prevent synchronization effects and reduce network congestion when multiple clients start simultaneously. The delay must be calculated using a random value within the specified range, and the client must not transmit the message until this delay period has elapsed. This requirement applies regardless of whether the Information-request is triggered by normal configuration needs or in response to a Reconfigure message, and must be implemented before proceeding with the transmission parameters defined in Section 15.

During the DHCPv6 Confirm exchange, when a client receives multiple Reply messages in response to its Confirm message, if at least one Reply message indicates a Success status (either explicitly through a Status Code option or implicitly by the absence of any error status), the client SHALL consider the addresses in the associated Identity Association (IA) as valid for the current network link and proceed to use them. In this case, the client MAY ignore any other Reply messages that indicate a NotOnLink status. This rule applies specifically when the client is verifying address validity after network movement or reconnection, and ensures that a single successful confirmation from any DHCPv6 server overrides conflicting NotOnLink indications from other servers.

This rule governs the client's behavior when validating its network location through the Confirm message exchange in DHCPv6. It applies specifically when the client receives only Reply messages containing a NotOnLink status code in response to its Confirm message, indicating that none of the server(s) consider the client's current network location valid for its configured addresses. Under these conditions, the client must initiate DHCP server discovery as specified in Section 18 to obtain new address configuration appropriate for its current network location. This rule applies only after the client has sent a Confirm message and received one or more Reply messages, all of which contain the NotOnLink status, with no Reply messages indicating Success status (either explicit or implicit).

When a DHCPv6 client detects that one or more assigned IPv6 addresses are already in use by another node, it MUST send a Decline message to report the address conflict. However, since the Decline mechanism applies only to address assignment and not to prefix delegation, the client MUST NOT include any IA_PD options (see Section 21.21) in the Decline message. This restriction applies specifically during the Decline message creation phase, following address conflict detection but prior to server retransmission procedures. The expected outcome is that the server will be notified only of conflicting addresses without affecting any delegated prefix bindings. This rule applies only when the client is operating in stateful address assignment mode and has received addresses through IA_NA or IA_TA options.

When a DHCPv6 client initiates a lease release by sending a Release message, it must generate a unique transaction ID and populate the "transaction-id" field with this value. This applies specifically during the Release message creation phase, following the setting of the "msg-type" field to RELEASE and preceding the inclusion of server/client identifiers and IA options. The transaction ID serves to correlate the Release message with its corresponding Reply from the server, ensuring proper message association in the exchange. This step is mandatory for all Release message transmissions and must be performed regardless of whether the release is initiated during normal operation or shutdown scenarios.

When a DHCPv6 client detects that one or more addresses allocated by a server are already in use by another node, it MUST include the server's identifier in a Server Identifier option (see Section 21.3) within the Decline message. This requirement applies specifically during the creation of the Decline message, which occurs after address conflict detection but before transmission. The Server Identifier option enables the receiving server to identify itself as the source of the original address allocation, ensuring proper handling of the declined addresses. This rule applies only when declining addresses (not prefixes) and requires that the client has previously received a valid Server Identifier from the server during the initial address assignment process.

When a DHCPv6 client initiates a Solicit message to discover available DHCP servers, it MUST include a Client Identifier option (see Section 21.2) to uniquely identify itself to the server. This requirement applies during the initial message construction phase before transmission, and the Client Identifier option must be present in every Solicit message regardless of the client's configuration state or requested parameters. The expected outcome is that servers can properly identify and track the client across subsequent message exchanges, enabling consistent address assignment and lease management. This rule applies unconditionally to all Solicit messages and is a prerequisite for valid protocol operation.

When a DHCPv6 client detects that one or more addresses assigned by a server via an IA_NA option are already in use on the local network (as determined through Duplicate Address Detection or other means), the client MUST send a Decline message to the server to report the address conflict. This rule applies during the address assignment phase after the client has received a Reply message containing addresses but before using those addresses for communication.

The client MUST construct the Decline message with the following requirements:
- Set msg-type to DECLINE with a generated transaction ID
- Include Client Identifier and Server Identifier options
- Include an Elapsed Time option indicating exchange duration
- Include IA_NA options containing only the conflicting addresses to be declined
- Exclude IA_PD options (as Decline is not used for prefix delegation)
- Avoid using the declined addresses as source addresses

The client MUST transmit the message using the retransmission parameters: IRT DEC_TIMEOUT, MRT 0, MRC DEC_MAX_RC, MRD 0. Upon sending the Decline, the client SHOULD retain all non-conflicting bindings and treat the failed address acquisition as if the binding was never received for Renew/Rebind purposes. The server's potential Reply with NoBinding status should not be treated as an error condition.

When a client detects that one or more addresses assigned by a DHCPv6 server are already in use by another node, it MUST send a Decline message to report the address conflict. In this message and all subsequent DHCPv6 transmissions, the client MUST NOT use any of the addresses it is declining as the source address. This requirement applies immediately upon detecting the conflict and persists until the client receives a valid binding update from the server. The client MUST instead use an alternative valid address from its bindings or a link-local address as the source. This prevents network disruption caused by transmitting from an address that may be actively used by another node and ensures proper handling of the decline procedure by the server.

When constructing a Solicit message during the initial DHCPv6 configuration exchange, the client MUST include an Elapsed Time option (Section 21.9) that measures the cumulative duration from the start of the current DHCP message exchange attempt. This requirement applies specifically to the Solicit message transmission phase and serves to inform DHCPv6 servers about the client's waiting time, enabling servers to prioritize responses for clients that have been waiting longer. The client must calculate the elapsed time starting from when it began attempting to complete the current DHCP exchange, and this option must be included regardless of whether this is the initial transmission or a retransmission. This rule applies unconditionally to all Solicit messages and is essential for proper server load balancing and client prioritization in the DHCPv6 protocol.

When a client sends a Decline message to report suspected address conflicts and receives a Reply message with a NoBinding status code, the client MUST NOT interpret this status as an error condition. This situation typically occurs when the server's response to an initial Decline message is lost, causing the client to retransmit the Decline, but the server no longer maintains the binding being declined. The client should continue normal operation without triggering error recovery procedures, retain all non-conflicting bindings, and proceed with standard Renew/Rebind message exchanges for remaining valid bindings. This rule applies specifically to DHCPv6 address assignment scenarios and does not apply to prefix delegation exchanges.

When a DHCPv6 client detects that one or more addresses assigned by a server are already in use by another node, it MUST construct a Decline message to report the address conflicts. As part of this message construction, the client MUST include in the "options" field the Identity Association (IA) options that contain the specific addresses being declined. Each IA option MUST contain only the addresses the client is declining due to conflict detection, while excluding any addresses from the same IA that remain valid and usable. This rule applies specifically during the Decline message creation phase and does not apply to prefix delegation scenarios (IA_PD options MUST NOT be included). The inclusion of these IA options enables the server to identify and revoke the conflicting address assignments while preserving non-conflicting bindings.

When a client sends a Decline message to report that one or more assigned addresses are already in use, it MUST NOT automatically send Release messages for other valid bindings it has received from the same server. This rule applies specifically during the address conflict resolution phase, after the client has detected address conflicts but before it proceeds with normal lease management operations. The expected outcome is that the client retains all non-conflicting bindings and continues to use them according to their normal lease lifetimes, while treating the declined addresses as if they were never allocated. This behavior ensures that address conflicts are resolved without unnecessarily disrupting other valid network configurations. The rule applies only when the client has successfully obtained multiple bindings from a server and subsequently identifies conflicts in a subset of those addresses.

When a DHCPv6 client detects that one or more assigned addresses are already in use, it initiates a Decline message exchange to inform the server. If the server's Reply message confirming the Decline is lost during transmission, the client MUST retransmit the Decline message according to the retransmission parameters (IRT DEC_TIMEOUT, MRT 0, MRC DEC_MAX_RC, MRD 0). In response to the retransmitted Decline, the server MAY send a Reply with a status of NoBinding, indicating it has no record of the binding being declined. The client MUST NOT interpret this NoBinding status as an error condition and SHOULD continue to retain all other valid bindings while treating the declined addresses as if they were never allocated. This rule applies specifically during the Decline message exchange phase and does not affect the client's handling of Renew or Rebind messages for non-conflicting bindings.

When a DHCPv6 client initiates a lease release by sending a Release message, it must generate a unique transaction ID and populate the "transaction-id" field with this value. This applies specifically during the Release message creation phase, following the setting of the "msg-type" field to RELEASE and preceding the inclusion of server/client identifiers and IA options. The transaction ID serves to correlate the Release message with its corresponding Reply from the server, ensuring proper message association in the exchange. This step is mandatory for all Release message transmissions and must be performed regardless of whether the release is initiated during normal operation or shutdown scenarios.

When a DHCPv6 client initiates a lease release by sending a Release message, it MUST include the Server Identifier option containing the unique identifier of the DHCPv6 server that originally allocated the lease(s) being released. This requirement applies specifically during the Release message construction phase, following the generation of the transaction ID and preceding the inclusion of Client Identifier and Elapsed Time options. The Server Identifier enables the receiving server to verify it was the original allocator of the specified leases, ensuring proper authorization and lease state management. This rule applies only when the client has valid lease bindings from a known server and has already ceased using all addresses/prefixes being released as mandated by the protocol.

When a client needs to release one or more active leases (addresses or delegated prefixes) that it no longer requires, it MUST initiate a Release message exchange by sending a Release message to the server that originally allocated the leases. This action applies during normal lease management operations or prior to shutdown. Before transmission, the client MUST have already ceased using all leases being released—for addresses, this means removing them from the interface; for delegated prefixes, advertising them with zero lifetimes in Router Advertisements. The client MUST include required options: msg-type set to RELEASE, a unique transaction ID, Server Identifier, Client Identifier, Elapsed Time, and IA options containing only the leases to be released. The client MUST NOT use any released address as the source address in the Release message or subsequent messages. The client SHOULD retransmit the Release if no Reply is received, though early termination is permitted in scenarios like power-down. If the server responds with a status of NoBinding, the client SHOULD NOT treat this as an error, as it may indicate a previously lost Reply. If the release fails, leases will expire naturally based on their valid lifetimes.

When a DHCPv6 client initiates a Solicit message to discover available DHCP servers, it MUST include a Client Identifier option (see Section 21.2) to uniquely identify itself to the server. This requirement applies during the initial message construction phase before transmission, and the Client Identifier option must be present in every Solicit message regardless of the client's configuration state or requested parameters. The expected outcome is that servers can properly identify and track the client across subsequent message exchanges, enabling consistent address assignment and lease management. This rule applies unconditionally to all Solicit messages and is a prerequisite for valid protocol operation.

When a DHCPv6 client initiates a lease release by sending a Release message, it must include in the "options" field all Identity Associations (IAs) that contain the specific leases being released. This applies after the client has already stopped using the leases (removed addresses from interfaces or invalidated delegated prefixes) and before transmitting the Release message. The client must ensure that only leases intended for release are included in these IAs, while excluding any leases it wishes to retain. This structured inclusion enables the server to accurately identify and deallocate the specified leases, ensuring proper resource management and preventing unintended lease termination.

When a DHCPv6 client initiates a Release message exchange to return a delegated prefix lease, it MUST first ensure the prefix is no longer in use on the network. Specifically, for delegated prefixes, this requires that the client has advertised the prefix in a Router Advertisement message with both Preferred Lifetime and Valid Lifetime set to 0, as defined in RFC4862 Section 5.5.3(e) and RFC7084 Section 4.3 requirement L-13. This action signals downstream nodes to deprecate the prefix and prevents further use before the Release message is sent. The client MUST complete this advertisement step prior to beginning the Release message exchange process and MUST NOT use any released addresses in subsequent communications. This ensures clean lease relinquishment and proper network resource management.

When constructing a Solicit message during the initial DHCPv6 configuration exchange, the client MUST include an Elapsed Time option (Section 21.9) that measures the cumulative duration from the start of the current DHCP message exchange attempt. This requirement applies specifically to the Solicit message transmission phase and serves to inform DHCPv6 servers about the client's waiting time, enabling servers to prioritize responses for clients that have been waiting longer. The client must calculate the elapsed time starting from when it began attempting to complete the current DHCP exchange, and this option must be included regardless of whether this is the initial transmission or a retransmission. This rule applies unconditionally to all Solicit messages and is essential for proper server load balancing and client prioritization in the DHCPv6 protocol.

During the DHCPv6 Release message exchange, when a client retransmits a Release message due to a lost Reply from the server, the server may respond with a Reply message containing a status of NoBinding. This status indicates that the server no longer has a binding record for the released leases, which can occur when the initial Release was successfully processed but the corresponding Reply was lost. The client MUST NOT interpret this NoBinding status as an error condition, as it does not signify a failure in the release process. Instead, the client should consider the release operation complete and proceed normally. This behavior applies specifically to Release message retransmissions and requires that the client has already stopped using all leases being released before initiating the Release exchange.

When a DHCPv6 client initiates a lease release by sending a Release message, it must implement retransmission mechanisms to handle potential message loss. The client should retransmit the Release message if no corresponding Reply message is received from the server within the expected timeframe. This retransmission behavior applies specifically during the Release message exchange process, after the client has already stopped using all leases being released (including removing addresses from interfaces and advertising delegated prefixes with zero lifetimes). The retransmission parameters should follow the protocol specification (IRT REL_TIMEOUT, MRT 0, MRC REL_MAX_RC, MRD 0), though implementations may terminate retransmission early in scenarios such as system power-down. If the server responds with a Reply indicating NoBinding status due to previous successful release, the client should not treat this as an error condition, as the fundamental objective of lease release has been achieved.

When a DHCPv6 client initiates a lease release by sending a Release message and does not receive a corresponding Reply from the server (indicating potential message loss), the client MUST retransmit the Release message according to the retransmission parameters specified in Section 15 (IRT REL_TIMEOUT, MRT 0, MRC REL_MAX_RC, MRD 0). This rule applies during the Release message exchange process after the client has already stopped using all leases being released and removed them from the interface. In response to a retransmitted Release, the server may reply with a status of NoBinding if it no longer has a binding record for the released leases. The client MUST NOT treat such a NoBinding status as an error condition, as this is a valid server response in this scenario. The retransmission mechanism ensures reliable lease release even in cases of network packet loss, while the NoBinding status handling prevents unnecessary error reporting when the server has already cleaned up the lease bindings.

When a DHCPv6 client sends a Renew message to extend the lifetimes of delegated prefixes assigned to its IA_PD bindings, it must include IA Prefix options (Section 21.22) encapsulated within the corresponding IA_PD options (Section 21.21) for each currently valid delegated prefix. This applies specifically during the Renew/Reply message exchange initiated at time T1 (or earlier under specific conditions) when the client seeks to renew existing prefix delegations obtained from the server. The client must only include prefixes that are currently valid (with non-zero valid lifetimes) and that were originally obtained from the server. This ensures the server can properly identify and process the prefix renewal requests for the specific IA_PD bindings.

When a DHCPv6 client sends a Renew message to extend the lifetimes of assigned addresses, it MUST include IA Address options (Section 21.6) encapsulated within the corresponding IA_NA (Section 21.4) and IA_TA (Section 21.5) options for all currently valid addresses assigned to those IAs. This requirement applies specifically during the Renew message exchange initiated at time T1 (or earlier under specific conditions) and only for addresses that remain valid (non-zero valid lifetime) and were originally obtained from the server. The client MUST NOT include addresses that are no longer valid or were not obtained from the server. This ensures the server can properly identify which specific address leases the client wishes to renew and enables the server to update lifetimes or reassign addresses as needed.

A client MUST initiate a Renew/Reply message exchange before the scheduled T1 timer expiration if both of the following conditions are met: (1) the client's link-local address that was previously used for communication with the DHCPv6 server becomes invalid, and (2) the client is configured to accept Reconfigure messages from the server. This early renewal ensures that the client maintains valid addressing information and remains reachable for server-initiated reconfiguration while its link-local address changes. The client MUST follow the standard Renew message construction rules, including proper IA option handling and server identification, but initiates this exchange ahead of the normal T1 schedule due to the address validity condition.

When a DHCPv6 client needs to extend the preferred and valid lifetimes for existing leases assigned to its Identity Associations (IAs) and potentially obtain new addresses or delegated prefixes, it MUST send a Renew message to the server that originally granted these leases. This rule applies during the T1 timer period, which is the designated renewal time controlled by the server, or earlier if specific conditions are met (such as the client's link-local address becoming invalid). The Renew message MUST include IA options (IA_NA, IA_TA, or IA_PD) containing IA Address options for assigned addresses and IA Prefix options for delegated prefixes, but MUST exclude any addresses or prefixes not obtained from the server or those with expired valid lifetimes. The client MUST also include Server Identifier, Client Identifier, and Elapsed Time options, set the message type to RENEW, generate a transaction ID, and follow specified transmission parameters. The expected outcome is the initiation of a Renew/Reply exchange to extend lease lifetimes and potentially acquire new resources, with the exchange terminating at the earliest T2 time, at which point the client transitions to the Rebind message exchange if renewal is unsuccessful.

When a DHCPv6 client needs to extend lease lifetimes for multiple Identity Associations (IAs) obtained from the same server, it SHOULD consolidate these renewals into a single Renew message exchange. Since each IA may have different T1 (renewal time) and T2 (rebinding time) values assigned by the server, the client MUST calculate global T1 and T2 values by selecting the earliest T1 and earliest T2 from among all active IAs. This ensures that the client initiates Renew message transmissions before any individual IA's T1 expires, and transitions to Rebind message exchanges before any individual IA's T2 expires. This rule applies during the lease renewal phase when the client has multiple active bindings from the same server, and ensures timely renewal of all leases while minimizing protocol overhead through consolidated message exchanges.

When initiating a DHCPv6 configuration exchange, during the creation of a Solicit message as specified in Section 18.2.1, the client MUST generate a unique transaction ID and populate the "transaction-id" field with this value. This applies specifically to the initial message in the DHCPv6 client-server exchange and serves to correlate requests with responses, ensuring proper message association throughout the transaction lifecycle. The transaction ID MUST be generated before any other message field population and MUST remain consistent for all messages within the same transaction sequence. This rule is mandatory for all Solicit message transmissions and is a prerequisite for valid DHCPv6 protocol operation.

When a DHCPv6 client initiates a Solicit message to discover available DHCP servers, it MUST include a Client Identifier option (see Section 21.2) to uniquely identify itself to the server. This requirement applies during the initial message construction phase before transmission, and the Client Identifier option must be present in every Solicit message regardless of the client's configuration state or requested parameters. The expected outcome is that servers can properly identify and track the client across subsequent message exchanges, enabling consistent address assignment and lease management. This rule applies unconditionally to all Solicit messages and is a prerequisite for valid protocol operation.

When a DHCPv6 client initiates a Renew message exchange to extend lease lifetimes for its IA bindings, it MUST include a Server Identifier option (see Section 21.3) that specifically identifies the server from which the client most recently obtained its current leases. This requirement applies during the Renew message transmission phase, which occurs at time T1 (or earlier if the client's link-local address becomes invalid) as part of the client's lease renewal process. The Server Identifier option enables the receiving server to authenticate the renewal request and associate it with the client's existing bindings, ensuring proper lease management and preventing unauthorized lease modifications. This rule applies only when the client has previously completed a successful DHCPv6 message exchange with a server and has valid IA bindings to renew.

When the earliest T1 timer among all IA bindings expires, the client MUST initiate a Renew/Reply message exchange with the server that originally granted the leases. The client constructs a Renew message containing IA options for all active bindings, including IA Address options for assigned addresses and IA Prefix options for delegated prefixes, along with required Server Identifier and Client Identifier options. This exchange aims to extend the preferred and valid lifetimes of existing leases before they expire. The client MUST use the earliest T1 value from all IA options to ensure timely renewal of all bindings from the same server simultaneously. If T1 was set to 0 by the server for IA_NA or IA_PD, or no T1 exists for IA_TA, the client may optionally send a Renew message at its discretion. The message transmission follows the retransmission parameters specified in Section 15, and the exchange terminates when the earliest T2 time is reached, at which point the client proceeds to the Rebind message exchange.

During the DHCPv6 lease renewal process, when a client needs to extend lease lifetimes for IAs (Identity Associations), it typically follows the T1 and T2 timers provided by the server in previous Reply messages. However, if the server previously set T1 or T2 to 0 for IA_NA or IA_PD associations, or if no T1/T2 times were provided for IA_TA associations, the client has discretionary authority to initiate a Renew message (when T1=0 or missing) or a Rebind message (when T2=0 or missing) respectively. This rule applies specifically when the client cannot rely on standard timer-based renewal triggers and must make an independent decision about when to contact the server for lease extension. The client must still follow all standard message construction requirements for Renew/Rebind messages, including proper IA option inclusion, transaction ID generation, and Server/Client Identifier options, while maintaining compliance with the protocol's transmission timing rules.

When constructing a Renew message to extend lease lifetimes for IAs, the client MUST only include addresses and prefixes in IA options that were previously obtained from the server and remain valid. Specifically, the client MUST NOT include any addresses or prefixes that were not originally assigned by the server or that have expired (indicated by a valid lifetime of 0). This rule applies during the Renew message creation phase when the client is attempting to extend existing leases, and ensures that the server only processes currently valid bindings, preventing attempts to renew unauthorized or expired resources.

When constructing a Solicit message during the initial DHCPv6 configuration exchange, the client MUST include an Elapsed Time option (Section 21.9) that measures the cumulative duration from the start of the current DHCP message exchange attempt. This requirement applies specifically to the Solicit message transmission phase and serves to inform DHCPv6 servers about the client's waiting time, enabling servers to prioritize responses for clients that have been waiting longer. The client must calculate the elapsed time starting from when it began attempting to complete the current DHCP exchange, and this option must be included regardless of whether this is the initial transmission or a retransmission. This rule applies unconditionally to all Solicit messages and is essential for proper server load balancing and client prioritization in the DHCPv6 protocol.

During the Renew message exchange, when the client includes IA Prefix options within IA_PD options to extend delegated prefix lifetimes or request new prefix delegations, the client SHOULD NOT set the "IPv6-prefix" field to 0 (indicating an unspecified prefix) unless the client is explicitly providing a prefix length hint to the server. This restriction applies specifically when the client is renewing existing prefix delegations or requesting new delegations through IA_PD options. The expected outcome is that servers receive meaningful prefix requests without ambiguous zero-value prefixes, except when clients intentionally provide prefix length hints using the "prefix-length" field. This rule applies only when the client includes IA Prefix options in Renew messages and does not affect IA Address options or other message types.

When constructing a Renew message, the client MUST include all relevant options required for the renewal process. This includes one or more IA options corresponding to the IAs whose lease lifetimes are to be extended, along with any other appropriate options such as IA Address options within IA_NA/IA_TA for assigned addresses, IA Prefix options within IA_PD for delegated prefixes, Server Identifier, Client Identifier, Elapsed Time, and Option Request options. This rule applies during the Renew message creation phase when the client initiates a renewal to extend lease lifetimes at time T1 or when required due to link-local address invalidation. The expected outcome is a properly formatted Renew message that enables the server to identify the client, verify existing bindings, and process lease renewal requests according to the DHCPv6 protocol specifications.

After sending a Solicit message and receiving one or more valid Advertise messages in response, the client must select one or more servers to proceed with in the DHCPv6 configuration exchange. This selection occurs during the address/prefix acquisition phase before sending Request messages. The client evaluates Advertise messages primarily by server preference value (higher values preferred), but may also consider advertised parameters such as available IA options and configuration settings. The client must store server information for state management and may maintain independent per-IA state machines for each selected server. Advertise messages containing no addresses or delegated prefixes (except for processing SOL_MAX_RT and INF_MAX_RT options) must be ignored, and such ignored messages must not trigger Solicit retransmission timer restarts.

When a DHCPv6 client receives an Advertise message that it chooses to ignore (for example, because the message contains no addresses or delegated prefixes as specified in the protocol), the client MUST NOT restart the Solicit retransmission timer. This rule applies specifically to ignored Advertise messages during the address/prefix acquisition phase and ensures that the client's retransmission behavior for Solicit messages remains consistent according to the existing timer schedule, preventing unnecessary delays in the configuration process.

When a DHCPv6 client sends a Renew message to extend lease lifetimes and obtain new configuration parameters, it MUST include an Option Request option (see Section 21.7) to explicitly request the SOL_MAX_RT option (see Section 21.24) for managing the maximum retransmission timeout, along with any other configuration options the client requires. This requirement applies specifically during the Renew message creation phase, following the inclusion of mandatory Server Identifier, Client Identifier, Elapsed Time, and IA options. The expected outcome is that the server will respond with the requested options in its Reply message, enabling the client to maintain proper configuration parameters and retransmission behavior. This rule applies only when the client is initiating a Renew/Reply message exchange, either at time T1 or earlier if the client's link-local address has become invalid and the client is willing to receive Reconfigure messages.

During the DHCPv6 client's processing of Advertise messages, even when an Advertise message contains a Status Code option indicating failure and will ultimately be discarded, the client MUST still extract and process any SOL_MAX_RT and INF_MAX_RT options present in that message. This requirement applies specifically to the message evaluation phase before final server selection, ensuring that retransmission timing parameters are captured from all received advertisements regardless of their overall validity. The processed SOL_MAX_RT and INF_MAX_RT values may be used to update the client's retransmission behavior, though the client should only update its operational values if all received Advertise messages containing these options specify consistent values. This rule ensures that critical timing configuration is not lost even when individual Advertise messages are rejected due to other failures.

When selecting among valid Advertise messages received in response to a Solicit message, the client SHOULD prefer messages with the highest server preference value. However, the client MAY override this preference and select a server with a lower preference value if that server advertises superior configuration parameters that better meet the client's requirements. Superior parameters may include a more suitable set of Identity Associations (IAs), such as IA_NA, IA_TA, or IA_PD options with more appropriate address allocations or prefix delegations, as well as other configuration options that align better with the client's operational needs. This selection flexibility applies during the initial server choice phase and may also be used when selecting alternate servers if the primary chosen server becomes unresponsive. The client must evaluate all advertised parameters against its configuration requirements before making this determination.

When a DHCPv6 client receives multiple valid Advertise messages during the server selection process, it SHOULD prioritize messages with the highest server preference value as the primary selection criterion. This preference-based selection occurs after the client has validated all received Advertise messages and before initiating subsequent configuration exchanges. However, the client MAY override this preference if a server with a lower preference value offers more suitable configuration parameters (such as better IA sets or configuration options). The client MUST continue to process SOL_MAX_RT and INF_MAX_RT options from all Advertise messages regardless of preference values, and MUST discard any message that contains no addresses or delegated prefixes (except for processing the aforementioned timing options). This rule applies specifically during the server selection phase following Advertise message reception and precedes the client's storage of server information and initiation of Request messages.

During the DHCPv6 client's processing of Advertise messages, when multiple valid messages are received and the client is selecting servers based on preference values and advertised parameters, the client MUST process any SOL_MAX_RT and INF_MAX_RT options present in each Advertise message, even if the message contains a failure Status Code and will be discarded. The client SHOULD update its SOL_MAX_RT and INF_MAX_RT configuration values only if all received Advertise messages that contained the corresponding option specify identical values for that option. If different values are advertised across messages, or if the option appears inconsistently, the client MUST revert to using the default values specified in Section 7.6. This ensures consistent timeout behavior for Solicit and Information-request message retransmissions across all server interactions.

During the Advertise message selection process following a Solicit message, the client MUST ignore any Advertise message that contains no addresses (IA Address options encapsulated in IA_NA or IA_TA options) and no delegated prefixes (IA Prefix options encapsulated in IA_PD options), as such messages provide no usable network configuration. However, even when ignoring such messages, the client MUST process any included SOL_MAX_RT and INF_MAX_RT options to update its retransmission timing parameters. This rule applies specifically to Advertise messages that lack both address assignments and prefix delegations, ensuring the client only considers servers offering meaningful network resources while maintaining proper retransmission behavior.

When a DHCPv6 client receives a valid Reply message in response to a Solicit (with Rapid Commit), Request, Confirm, Renew, Rebind, or Information-request message, it MUST process any SOL_MAX_RT and INF_MAX_RT options present in the Reply, regardless of whether the message contains a Status Code option indicating a failure. This requirement applies specifically to the processing of these maximum retransmission time options, which govern client retransmission behavior for subsequent messages. The client must extract and apply the values from these options even when the overall transaction is considered unsuccessful due to a failure status code, ensuring proper retransmission timing controls are maintained for future DHCPv6 exchanges.
req_type: Solicit, Request, Confirm, Renew, Rebind, Information-request

rule: When a DHCPv6 client receives a valid Reply message in response to any of the following client-initiated messages: Solicit (with Rapid Commit option), Request, Confirm, Renew, Rebind, or Information-request, the client MUST first extract and examine the top-level Status Code option if present. This rule applies during the message processing phase immediately after message validation and before any other configuration parameter processing. The client must handle the Status Code option regardless of the message type that triggered the Reply, as it indicates the server's processing outcome for the client's original request. If no Status Code option is present, the client proceeds with normal Reply processing based on the original message type. This extraction is mandatory and serves as the initial step in determining whether the server successfully processed the client's request or encountered specific conditions that require special handling.
req_type: Solicit, Request, Confirm, Renew, Rebind, Information-request

rule: When a DHCPv6 client receives a valid Reply message containing a Status Code option with the value UseMulticast, this indicates that the server requires all subsequent client messages to be sent via multicast rather than unicast. The client MUST record this requirement and ensure that all future messages directed to this server are transmitted through the same network interface on which the Reply was received, using multicast addressing. This rule applies specifically during the processing of Reply messages received in response to Solicit (with Rapid Commit), Request, Confirm, Renew, Rebind, or Information-request messages, and takes effect immediately upon receipt of such a status code. The client MUST resend the original message using multicast as part of complying with this directive.

When a DHCPv6 client receives a valid Reply message containing a Status Code option with the value UnspecFail, this indicates that the server encountered an unspecified failure condition preventing it from processing the client's original message. This rule applies specifically during the client's processing of Reply messages received in response to Solicit (with Rapid Commit), Request, Confirm, Renew, Rebind, or Information-request messages. Upon receiving an UnspecFail status, the client may retransmit the original message to the same server but MUST implement rate limiting and duration constraints as specified in Section 14.1. The client must continue to process SOL_MAX_RT and INF_MAX_RT options even when encountering this failure status. This behavior allows for graceful handling of transient server failures while preventing excessive retransmission attempts.
req_type: Solicit, Request, Confirm, Renew, Rebind, Information-request

rule: When processing Reply messages, the general behavior for handling missing configuration options (where previously received options are absent in subsequent Renew/Rebind/Information-request Replies) does not apply to Identity Association (IA) options. IA options follow specialized processing rules defined in the subsequent protocol section, which govern their lifecycle management, address/prefix assignments, and state transitions independently from other configuration options. This exception ensures IA options maintain consistent binding states and adhere to specific renewal/rebinding semantics separate from general configuration parameter handling.

When a client has previously received a configuration option in a Reply message and subsequently sends a Renew, Rebind, or Information-request message requesting that same option, if the option is absent in the new Reply, the client SHOULD cease using the previously received configuration information. This rule applies during stateful configuration updates when the server intentionally omits a previously provided option, indicating that the configuration is no longer valid or supported. The expected outcome is that the client reverts to the relevant default state as if the option had never been received. This behavior does not apply to IA options, which are handled separately. If ceasing use of the configuration is not feasible (e.g., due to system constraints and no external impact), the client MAY retain the values, but MUST prioritize updated values when available and remain open to alternative configuration sources.

When a DHCPv6 client receives a valid Reconfigure message on UDP port 546 from an interface where it has previously obtained configuration, this message serves as a trigger to initiate a new message exchange. The client MUST respond with the message type specified in the Reconfigure Message option (Renew, Rebind, or Information-request) and ignore the transaction-id field. During the ongoing exchange, the client MUST discard any additional Reconfigure messages and only process the next one after completing the current transaction. This mechanism ensures the client updates its configuration while preventing duplicate processing of reconfigure triggers.

When a DHCPv6 client receives a valid Reconfigure message from a server, it must immediately initiate the corresponding message exchange (sending a Renew, Rebind, or Information-request message as specified by the Reconfigure Message option, with retransmissions as needed). During this ongoing exchange, the client MUST ignore all subsequent Reconfigure messages received on the same interface until the current transaction completes successfully. This rule applies specifically to the period between receiving the initial Reconfigure trigger and completing the resulting configuration update exchange, ensuring that duplicate or retransmitted Reconfigure messages do not cause redundant transactions while maintaining the protocol's efficiency and correct operation.

A client MUST listen for and accept Reconfigure messages sent to UDP port 546 on interfaces where it has previously obtained configuration information through DHCP. This rule applies when the server initiates a reconfiguration event at any time after the client has acquired initial configuration. Upon receiving a valid Reconfigure message, the client MUST respond with the appropriate message type (Renew, Rebind, or Information-request) as specified in the Reconfigure Message option, while ignoring the transaction-id field. The client MUST ignore all subsequent Reconfigure messages until the current message exchange is complete, and SHOULD log reconfiguration events since they may affect application-layer programs. This mechanism ensures the client can efficiently respond to server-initiated configuration updates while preventing redundant exchanges from duplicate or delayed messages.

When a DHCPv6 client receives a valid Reconfigure message on UDP port 546 for an interface with previously acquired DHCP configuration, it must respond with one of three message types as specified by the Reconfigure Message option: a Renew message, a Rebind message, or an Information-request message. This rule applies immediately upon receipt of the Reconfigure message, which acts as a trigger for the client to initiate a new configuration exchange. The client must ignore the transaction-id field in the Reconfigure message and discard any subsequent Reconfigure messages received while the exchange is in progress. This ensures that the client completes one configuration update cycle per Reconfigure trigger, maintaining protocol efficiency while allowing for necessary retransmissions by the server.

When a DHCPv6 client receives a valid Reconfigure message on UDP port 546 from an interface where it has previously acquired configuration, it MUST ignore the "transaction-id" field value in that message. This rule applies immediately upon receipt of the Reconfigure message, regardless of the transaction-id value, as the client initiates a new transaction by sending a Renew, Rebind, or Information-request message based on the Reconfigure Message option. The client MUST discard any additional Reconfigure messages received while the reconfiguration transaction is in progress, ensuring that only the first valid Reconfigure message triggers the exchange. This behavior prevents transaction-id validation conflicts and ensures predictable reconfiguration initiation, even if duplicate or retransmitted Reconfigure messages arrive with different transaction-id values.

When a client receives a Reply message in response to a Renew, Rebind, or Information-request message containing a requested configuration option with a value that differs from what was previously received, the client SHOULD apply the updated value to its configuration as soon as possible. This rule applies specifically to non-IA configuration options (such as NTP servers or domain names) and takes precedence over the previous configuration state. The client must first validate that the Reply message contains no error status codes (other than SOL_MAX_RT or INF_MAX_RT processing requirements) and that the option is explicitly present in the current Reply. The expected outcome is that the client's operational configuration reflects the most recent valid values provided by the DHCPv6 server, ensuring dynamic configuration consistency without requiring client reinitialization.
